preamble: The voiced uvular fricative /ʁ/ — the standard French rhotic — is regularly deleted in colloquial speech when it stands as the second member of a word-final obstruent-liquid cluster. In words like 'quatre' or 'autre', the post-obstruent /ʁ/ drops, simplifying the cluster. The deletion is variable and register-sensitive: it is rare in careful speech but reaches very high rates in casual Parisian French, where it is favoured by a following consonant.
phoneticEffects: Lenition, Consonant Deletion, Cluster Simplification, Elision

examples.note: 'quatre' (four): word-final post-obstruent /ʁ/ deleted in casual speech; one of the lexical items most prone to cluster reduction.
'autre' (other): the final /ʁ/ of the /tʁ/ cluster is dropped.
'ministre' (minister): /ʁ/ deleted from the word-final /stʁ/ cluster; Brand & Ernestus (2021) record reductions of this cluster up to [mis].

sources: Brand, S. & Ernestus, M. (2021). Reduction of word-final obstruent-liquid-schwa clusters in Parisian French. Corpus Linguistics and Linguistic Theory, 17(1), 249-285.

Boughton, Z. (2013). Social class, cluster simplification and following context: Sociolinguistic variation in word-final post-obstruent liquid deletion in French. Journal of French Language Studies, 23.
